title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered with animal rescue projects. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Anna, The Intake Assistant
Anna helped at the rescue centre on Wednesday evenings, when new animals usually arrived after work from several nearby towns. She had imagined cuddling frightened cats, but the first lessons were less sentimental. [47]Before any animal entered the building, she had to disinfect the isolation cages and record exactly which cleaning solution had been used.[/47] At first, that felt excessive, until a vet explained how quickly infection could spread. [48]Her experience as a nurse helped her recognise signs of dehydration in dogs that looked merely tired to other volunteers.[/48] She was careful not to give medical advice, but she knew when to call staff quickly. Anna says the hardest part was not becoming attached during the first forty-eight hours. She learned to speak softly, move slowly and accept that progress might be invisible. Her reward was seeing animals begin to sleep with their heads down, no longer listening for danger.
texts.B:
### Leo, The Transport Driver
Leo became a volunteer driver almost by accident one rainy morning. [49]While waiting at his vet’s reception, he overheard an appeal for someone with a van to collect animals from distant pounds.[/49] He offered one Saturday and soon found his weekends mapped around carriers, blankets and fuel stops. [50]He was surprised by the number of forms required before an animal could be moved from one local authority area to another.[/50] Missing a signature could delay a rescue by a day. Leo liked the quiet concentration of driving because nervous animals often settled when the radio was off. He also learned to plan routes with emergency clinics marked in advance. The journeys were not glamorous; sometimes the van smelled terrible for hours, and he carried spare shirts in the glove compartment. Still, he remembers a trembling greyhound placing one paw against the carrier door as if testing whether people could finally be trusted.
texts.C:
### Mei, The Foster Carer
Mei fostered small animals in the spare room of her flat after her children left home. She began with kittens, thinking they would be easy compared with dogs, but quickly discovered that frightened animals read a room differently from people. [51]After two kittens spent an entire afternoon wedged behind a hot radiator, she rearranged the furniture so every hiding place was safe and reachable.[/51] The change made feeding and cleaning calmer for everyone. Mei kept detailed notes on appetite, play and sleep, which later helped the adoption team match animals with suitable families. [52]Her most difficult moment was handing back the first rabbit she had nursed, even though she knew the new owners were perfect.[/52] She cried in the lift afterwards, feeling both proud and empty. Over time, she learned that fostering meant loving animals temporarily and honestly. Each goodbye created space for the next creature that needed a quiet beginning.
texts.D:
### Sam, The Release Monitor
Sam regularly worked with a wildlife group that returned healed birds and hedgehogs to suitable places. He joined after finding an injured owl near the road outside his grandmother’s house. [53]That single rescue made him want to understand what happened after wild animals left the clinic.[/53] Release days required more preparation than he expected, especially in busy suburbs. [54]He often waited until late evening because some animals had to be set free when traffic, dogs and human noise were at their lowest.[/54] Sam used a torch covered with red film and spoke only when necessary to avoid startling the animal. [55]He kept a map of safe release points, marking water, shelter and nearby roads before each decision was approved.[/55] The responsibility made him nervous, especially when an animal hesitated at the carrier door. [56]He is now training two new monitors, because release work cannot stop whenever one volunteer is busy.[/56]

questions.question: mentions a strict cleaning task that had to be completed before new arrivals came in?
questions.explanation: Anna disinfected isolation cages and recorded the cleaning solution before any animal entered.

questions.question: says professional healthcare experience helped them notice a physical warning sign?
questions.explanation: Anna’s nursing experience helped her recognise dehydration in dogs.

questions.question: offered to help after hearing a request while waiting for an appointment?
questions.explanation: Leo overheard an appeal while waiting at the vet’s reception.

questions.question: was surprised by official documents needed before transporting animals between areas?
questions.explanation: Leo was surprised by the forms required to move animals between local authority areas.

questions.question: changed a room after animals hid somewhere potentially unsafe?
questions.explanation: Mei rearranged the furniture after kittens hid behind a hot radiator.

questions.question: found it emotionally difficult to return an animal they had cared for temporarily?
questions.explanation: Mei struggled to hand back the first rabbit she had nursed.

questions.question: became interested in the work after a particular injured wild animal was found?
questions.explanation: Sam joined after finding an injured owl near the road.

questions.question: timed the work to reduce disturbance from people, pets and vehicles?
questions.explanation: Sam waited until late evening when traffic, dogs and human noise were lowest.

questions.question: recorded geographical details before choosing where animals should be returned?
questions.explanation: Sam kept a map of safe release points with water, shelter and road details.

questions.question: is preparing others so that the activity does not depend on one person’s availability?
questions.explanation: Sam is training two new monitors so release work can continue.
